nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving to still forest streams, creek margins, and pools with dense vegetation and leaf litter; often over sandy or muddy substrates with submerged roots and woody debris

decorPreferences: caves and cave-like structures (coconut shells, clay pots, PVC tubes) for spawning
dense planting with broad-leaved plants (Anubias, Echinodorus)
driftwood and roots for territorial delineation
leaf litter (dried Indian almond or oak leaves) to acidify water and provide cover

diet.notes: Readily accepts high-quality small sinking pellets, micro-pellets, and flake food. Benefits from regular offerings of small live or frozen foods: brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworms, and micro-worms. Wild fish forage on small invertebrates, micro-crustaceans, insect larvae, worms, and organic detritus in the substrate and leaf litter. A varied diet with protein-rich items promotes conditioning for breeding and enhances female coloration.

goodWith: small tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
avoidWith: other Pelvicachromis species (hybridization risk with P. pulcher; territorial conflict with any krib)
very small nano fish when breeding pair is established (fry-guarding pair becomes highly territorial)

imageCandidates.notes: Neale Monks; 799x411. Sharp full lateral view of a vibrant male showing black lateral stripe, iridescent flanks, bright yellow throat/belly, blue-ringed eye, and colourful purple/red-streaked anal and lyre caudal fins - strongest diagnostic display.
Ged (commonswiki); 572x400. In-tank shot of a coloured individual with violet-pink flank sheen, yellow throat, dorsal ocellus and pink anal fin; whole fish in frame, slightly lower resolution.

summary: The Striped Kribensis (Pelvicachromis taeniatus) is a West African dwarf cichlid from Nigeria and Cameroon, closely related to the common Kribensis. Multiple locale color forms exist — Nigeria Red, Moliwe, Wouri, Kienke, Lobé Red, and Bipindi — each with distinct coloration. Hybridization with P. pulcher has been reported in the trade, making pure-line sourcing important.
careNotes:
The Striped Kribensis is a West African dwarf cichlid (Pelvicachromis taeniatus) from coastal rivers of Nigeria and Cameroon, closely related to the more commonly kept P. pulcher. Multiple named locale populations exist, each associated with distinct coloration: 'Nigeria Red' and 'Lobé Red' display vivid red-orange bellies in females; 'Moliwe', 'Wouri', 'Kienke', and 'Bipindi' each have subtly different stripe and iridescent-patch patterns. Locale purity matters greatly — P. taeniatus has been reported to hybridize with P. pulcher in the trade, producing intermediate offspring. Sourcing from reputable breeders who maintain locale-specific lines is strongly recommended.

An 80 L aquarium with a footprint of at least 80 cm length is suitable for a pair; larger tanks (100–120 L) are preferred if kept alongside dither fish. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 5.5–7.0, gH 1–12) closely mimics their forested stream habitat; many keepers use RO water with remineralisation or Indian almond leaf extract to achieve soft conditions. Spawning success is significantly improved at the softer end of the range (pH 6.0–6.5, gH 2–6). Temperature 23–28°C.

Caves are essential for this species, which spawns exclusively inside enclosed spaces. Provide one cave per female and additional visual barriers to reduce aggression. Males establish territories around caves; multiple caves in a longer tank allow the pair to rotate sites. Dither fish (small schooling tetras that move through the open water and signal to the cichlids that the area is safe) help reduce shyness and distribute aggression. Territorial intensity increases sharply when a pair is spawning or guarding fry; dither fish and tankmate selection must account for this.
breedingNotes: The Striped Kribensis is a monogamous, cave-spawning cichlid that tends to form long-term pair bonds. Conditioning with high-protein live and frozen foods over 2–3 weeks typically triggers spawning. The female selects and defends a cave, displaying intensified belly coloration (often deep red or violet) to the male during courtship. Eggs are deposited on the cave ceiling or walls; a typical clutch ranges from 40–80 pinkish-white eggs. Both parents share incubation duties; eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 25–26°C, and fry become free-swimming after a further 5–7 days. Parental care is generally attentive — both fish shepherd fry within a defended zone for 4–6 weeks. First foods include freshly hatched brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii, the tiny free-swimming larvae), micro-worms, and powdered fry foods. Sex determination in P. taeniatus shows temperature-dependence at some locales; slightly cooler temperatures (24–25°C) are often reported to favour female-biased clutches. Males are larger and more elongate; females are rounder, smaller, and show a distinctive iridescent violet-pink patch on the abdomen when in breeding condition.

fish.breedingNotes: The Striped Kribensis spawns inside enclosed caves and tends to form monogamous pairs. A typical clutch of 40–80 eggs is deposited on the cave ceiling or walls, and both parents share guarding duties through hatching and into the free-swimming fry stage, typically for 4–6 weeks. First foods for the fry include Artemia nauplii and micro-worms. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5) is generally reported to improve spawning success markedly.
